papers.title: A Neurosymbolic Approach for Landslide Risk Assessment
papers.conferenceTitle: Proceedings of the 6th National Conference on Artificial Intelligence (Ital-IA 2026)
papers.abstract:
Landslide risk assessment remains a critical challenge, where current predictive models are still far from providing sufficiently reliable support for high-impact decisions affecting people, infrastructure, and territory. 
In this work, we propose a neurosymbolic architecture for landslide risk assessment that combines two neural pipelines: 
 one for high-resolution spatial \emph{susceptibility mapping} and one for ground deformation forecasting from InSAR time series, with a symbolic layer of strategic reasoning based on Intuitionistic Alternating-time Temporal Logic (IATL). 
IATL is well suited to this scenario because it models the progressive refinement of information and distinguishes between what is not yet proved and what is known to be false. Through an illustrative example, we show how the symbolic layer can extend the neural component in two ways: by supporting decisions in poorly represented scenarios and by filtering physically implausible outputs. The result is a decision-support framework in which predictions are complemented by formally checkable strategic properties and inspectable reasoning traces.

papers.title: Gaussian Process and Kalman Baselines for Bradyseismic Signal Extraction from GNSS Time Series at Campi Flegrei
papers.conferenceTitle: XXXIV Italian Workshop on Neural Networks
papers.abstract: Monitoring ground deformation at Campi Flegrei with GNSS requires separating the slow bradyseismic deformation signal from the trend, the seasonality, and the stochastic noise that overwhelm it. This work addresses this problem on an hourly dataset of seven GNSS stations in baseline configuration, three components each, spanning approximately four years of observations. We present a preprocessing chain that removes the deterministic components and the measurement artifacts and characterizes the residual noise as power-law plus white noise. We then construct two inference baselines for extracting the deformation signal: a Gaussian Process in batch formulation and a state-space Kalman filter, both grounded in the same generative model, so as to isolate the effect of the inference engine alone. The comparison, carried out on the residual spectrum, on the estimated noise parameters, and on the extracted signal, shows that the two baselines preserve the spectral slope to the third decimal place, agree on the power-law noise correction factor, and extract the same deformation signal, with sub-millimeter differences against a signal of tens-of-centimeters magnitude. This agreement holds despite the two methods differing in kernel, regularization, temporal resolution, and prior distribution, and indicates that, for these data, the extracted deformation signal is robust to the choice of estimation method.

papers.title: Machine Learning and Explainable Deep Learning for Forecasting Landslide Deformation using EGMS Time Series
papers.venueFull: Preprint Journal Paper, Applied Soft Computing
papers.abstract: Reliable forecasting of slow-moving landslide deformation remains challenging due to non-stationarity, noise, missing observations (gaps), and the cumulative nature of satellite-based displacement products. In this study, the time axis is defined on a fixed 12-day cadence, so the one-step forecast corresponds to a consistent 12-day horizon. Raw cumulative sequences are transformed into robust learning inputs through point-wise normalization using statistics computed exclusively from the training interval, local linear detrending, slope-encoded auxiliary channels, and outlier suppression. A hybrid model combining multi-scale convolutional filters, stacked Bidirectional LSTM layers, and a self-attention block is trained in an incremental next-step formulation, reducing drift accumulation while enabling short-term deformation forecasting. Evaluation on a strictly held-out temporal interval demonstrates stable generalization across heterogeneous kinematic regimes. Gradient-based attribution indicates that the network primarily relies on recent temporal states, yielding physically interpretable relevance patterns. Overall, the proposed framework provides a reproducible baseline for large-scale ground-motion forecasting and supports early evolution tracking of slow-moving landslide processes.

papers.title: An Intuitionistic Version of Alternating-Time Temporal Logic
papers.conferenceTitle: Proceedings of the 22nd International Conference on Principles of Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KR 2025)
papers.abstract: Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) are essential for modelling strategic interactions between multiple agents, often involving partial information. Managing this partial information is crucial for accurate decision-making and strategy optimization. However, partial information combined with perfect recall strategies renders verifying strategic properties undecidable. Intuitionism, a form of partial information which has not yet been explored in the context of MAS, introduces a novel perspective. In this paper, we propose Intuitionistic Alternating Time Temporal Logic (IATL), an extension of ATL that incorporates intuitionistic logic, providing a specialized representation of imperfect information. We define its syntax, semantics, and key structural properties. Additionally, we propose a PTIME-complete algorithm for IATL model checking, supported by benchmarks demonstrating its efficiency.

papers.title: An Intuitionistic Version of Computation Tree Logic
papers.conferenceTitle: Proceedings of the European Conference on Multi-Agent Systems (EUMAS 2025)
papers.abstract: Model checking plays a central role in the formal verification of dynamic systems. However, classical temporal logics such as CTL assume a complete and absolute perspective on truth, which limits their expressiveness in contexts where partial information and constructivity are essential. In this paper, we propose Intuitionistic Computation Tree Logic (ICTL), a novel temporal logic that integrates the semantics of CTL with the constructive principles of intuitionistic logic. ICTL adopts a birelational semantic framework which models the interaction between temporal evolution and the growth of knowledge over time. We formally define the syntax and semantics of ICTL, analyze its foundational properties, and show that key fixed-point characterizations of classical CTL hold in this setting. We then present an efficient model checking algorithm for the novel logic, maintaining the same PTIME complexity of classical CTL. ICTL is particularly well-suited for verifying properties in systems where traceability, information growth, and constructive reasoning are crucial, such as medical monitoring, geophysical data analysis, and database auditing. We validate our theoretical contributions through the extension of VITAMIN, a verification tool for Multi-Agent Systems, to support ICTL. Benchmarks confirm the practical tractability of ICTL model checking, reinforcing its potential for constructive temporal verification in real-world settings.

theses.title: An Intuitionistic Version of Computation Tree Logic
theses.venueFull: Master thesis manuscript 2024, University of Naples Federico II
theses.abstract: My thesis presents an intuitionistic version of Computation Tree Logic (CTL), known as Intuitionistic Computation Tree Logic (ICTL). I focused my efforts on the development and understanding of ICTL, which merges intuitionistic logic with CTL. A distinctive feature of ICTL is its birelational models and syntax that clearly distinguishes between existential and universal quantifications. In my research, I outlined the syntax and semantics of ICTL, examined the properties of birelational models, and verified the satisfiability of ICTL formulas. I explored the differences between CTL and ICTL, particularly the validity of fixed-point equivalences, introducing a new version of ICTL with an additional condition on the models to overcome limitations of the original proposal. I developed an efficient model checking algorithm for this new version of ICTL, highlighting its computational complexity and practical applications in constructively verifying system properties. In my thesis, I emphasized the importance of ICTL in verifying properties across various contexts, including open systems, and highlighted its potential for addressing more complex verification tasks compared to classical CTL. I discussed ICTL's application in different scenarios such as medical monitoring, database management, and agricultural land monitoring, emphasizing its ability to ensure persistent knowledge and information traceability over time. Finally, I presented an extension of VITAMIN, a prototype designed for formal verification of Multi-Agent Systems (MAS), highlighting its modularity, versatility, and ease of extension to accommodate various logics and models in property verification. I provided benchmarks to demonstrate the efficiency of the ICTL model checking process in VITAMIN.
